narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and an LSAT score of 35, you've put together a respectable profile for law school, including plans to attend full-time and coming from a middle-income family background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 56.8%—just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction stems from how your academic record stacks up against patterns seen in thousands of past students, focusing on key indicators of bar success. The most influential factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the typical student's rank of around the 5th or 6th decile, signaling weaker performance in later undergraduate years when compared to peers. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA, while close to the average of 3.21, actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction slightly toward passing, as it showed consistency in your overall college record. Your Year 1 decile of 2 had a smaller but still negative influence, again highlighting early academic struggles relative to the average student's stronger start in the 5th or 6th decile. These decile rankings carried the most weight because they reflect your position among classmates, which strongly correlates with bar exam outcomes in the model's analysis of historical data. Your LSAT, though a bit below the average of 36.7, and full-time status—common among 93% of students—played lesser roles here. In essence, while your profile isn't far off from many applicants, the combination of lower decile placements outweighed your strengths, tipping the model toward concern about first-time bar passage. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing targeted academic improvement to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through a post-baccalaureate program, and aim to retake the LSAT for a higher score. Gathering stronger letters of recommendation or relevant experience could also help in reapplying to schools with more holistic reviews. With focused effort on these areas, you can reshape your profile to align better with successful bar passers.
